original_abstract:
This chapter examines the multifaceted factors influencing the effective adoption and ethical implementation of artificial intelligence (AI) across the African continent. The study begins by contextualizing the concept of "Responsible AI," specifically investigating what this framework means for technologies developed within and for African societies. Through a comprehensive analysis, the authors identify critical systemic hurdles—such as digital illiteracy, a shortage of local specialized talent, infrastructure deficits, and significant governmental barriers—that currently impede progress. 

Beyond identifying challenges, the chapter provides an in-depth survey of the African AI startup and research ecosystem, highlighting key organizations of interest while noting concerning trends regarding data sovereignty and foreign technological influence. By envisioning a future for AI that is "for Africans, by Africans," the chapter concludes with actionable recommendations designed to foster a responsible, inclusive, and sustainable AI landscape that aligns with local values and socioeconomic needs.
humanized_abstract:
Chapter 8 Artificial Intelligence in Africa An analysis of factors that can affect its successful deployment and ethical application This chapter investigated the factors that may potentially influence the successful take-up and ethical application of artificial intelligence (AI) on the African continent. We started by reflecting upon what “Responsible AI” means for AI developed locally in Africa or for its context. In an empirical analysis we investigate barriers at four levels of society namely: (1) education and skills at the level of end-users/consumers, (2) skills at the level of experts and programmers in Africa, (3) ICT infrastructure in Africa, and (4) regulatory, policy and legislation environments of individual African countries.

This chapter identifies the challenges that pertain to the use of AI on the African continent. It carries out an in-depth survey of the African AI startup and research ecosystem by listing key organisations of interest. It also examines challenges related to data sovereignty and foreign AI technologies. In the end, the chapter posits the vision of an AI future that is “For Africans, By Africans” and provides recommendations aimed at ensuring that such a future is not only responsible but also inclusive and sustainable in a way that fits the context of Africa.

This chapter examines the multifaceted factors influencing the effective adoption and ethical implementation of artificial intelligence (AI) across the African continent.
